speaker: interviewer
text: Today is November 2nd, 1995. We are here with Michael Librach in Scarsdale, New York in the United States. We're speaking in English. My name is Terry Toll.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is November 2nd, 1995. We are here in Scarsdale, New York with Michael Librach. We're speaking in English. And my name is Terry Toll.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good morning. Could you give us your full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Michael Librach.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-I-B-R-A-C-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does the name Librach mean something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I like to think that it comes from libracha, which means blessing in Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Has there ever been a change in your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. That's the name that my father had and his father before him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Michael. That was changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it before?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Poland, and I had a very Polish name, Mieczyslaw. I had a middle name at that time, Zenon. Mieczyslaw Zenon was my-- my Polish name. I also had a Hebrew name, Moshe Zelman.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did your name change from Mieczyslaw to Michael?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was gradual. I was imprisoned in Germany with the French Army, and the French called me Michel. So it was from Michel, when I came to the United States, I made it Michael.

speaker: interviewer
text: When is your birthday?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birthday is February 16. I was born in 1917.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in a Lodz, Poland, which we pronounce 'Woodj.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you always live here until the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived in Lodz, except for one year that I spent in France studying. I lived in Lodz, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Jacob, Jacob Librach.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall when his birthday was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do, November 2nd. As a matter of fact, today would be his 120th birthday. He was born in 1875.

speaker: interviewer
text: So about how old was he when the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he was-- now, let's see. From '75 that's 25. Plus 39 is 64. He was 64 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe your father as you remember him in Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: The most important thing about my father was his gentleness and his goodness. He was a very good man. You can say that, you know, this was what permeated his personality, was being good to people and nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Dorota, which is equivalent to English Dorothy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: D-O-R-O-T-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was her birthday?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her birthday was October 13, and she was born in 1883.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she would have been about how old when the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, eight years less than my father, so it would be 56.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what memories do you have of your mother from when you were living in Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- it's a very peculiar thing. It affected very much my family life in Lodz. My mother, way before I was born, was a-- was a cancer victim. She had a cancer in her leg, and her leg was amputated. As a matter of fact, twice after the birth of my first sister. And in those years, it was not a very-- very well-known occurrence. Very few people had cancer in those days. And therefore, her family kind of mobilized around her. And she was the main-- the main person in the family. Everybody was taking care of poor Dorothy, and that affected her personality. So she was very self-centered, just the opposite of what my father was. And that probably affected the way we came out. Anyhow, I was born. I was the third child. I was born-- she already had an artificial leg, which was always in my childhood a great mystery to me, you know. I haven't seen that leg until here in America when she was in a home, and that-- an extra leg was placed in my basement for safekeeping. But this affected my life quite a bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were your sisters? You mentioned there were two other children.

speaker: interviewee
text: My older sister was Nina who was 11 years older than me. And Nina was-- I don't know. She was 11 years old than me. I had very little to do with her. Although little later on in life, we separated part of our apartment and gave it to her and her husband. So the closeness was there. But I was very close with her child, with her son, my nephew. We were not that far away from each other in age, so there was very great closeness. I was teaching him sports, and so on, and so forth. So that affected me very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Jich, which is a name-- Polish name is Jich. And that doesn't translate into English.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your second sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My second sister, Mary, is still alive here in America. And she was three years older, and she is three years older than me. And that-- there was a little more closeness, of course, because of the age difference. But while-- usually, an older brother and younger sister are much closer than the other way around, because her social group was different than mine and so on. So that's-- you know, and that persisted. Up-- right now, we live pretty far away from each other. She's in Silver Spring, Maryland. I am in Scarsdale. So you know, we speak on the phone once in a while but that's about All.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you could describe the ethics or lesson that you came away from this family experience in Lodz with, what would you describe it as?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my father was very socially-minded. Belonged to very many organizations helping people. And he was a Zionist also of the Trumpeldor branch of Zionism. You know, Jabotinsky and so on and-- which was the very aggressive Zionist movement. And I think that it's the feeling for other people, for other people's rights, and for other people's needs that permeated my life and comes from-- from that. My father had much greater influence on my life than my mother had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other relatives who lived nearby that you grew up with who hadn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The way we lived in Poland, in Lodz, there was a big family building. In the back of all the building were-- were factories which my family owned. And the whole building was occupied. The front of the building were apartments, were occupied by family. My grandparents were on the top floor and one of my mother's brothers. We were on the second floor with another brother of my mother. And downstairs were the offices. So this part of family, my mother's family, we lived very closely to. And you know, all the holidays and so on were together in my grandparents' house. Although, my grandfather died when I was six, which of course, changed it very much. And my father's family-- he had two sisters. And they lived outside of Lodz. They didn't live in Lodz. One lived in Berlin, and the other one lived in a small town in Poland. So we didn't see them as often. And I don't even recall my father's parents. I never met them. So you know, this is how the family. I came from them-- my mother's part of family was close by, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of kid were you growing up in Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rather nasty, and naughty, and so on. My school years, you know, I tried to get away with. I was one of those guys who could do much better at all times. And my father always used to go to all the conferences in school and coming with the same verdict always. You know, that I could do much better. Why don't I work a little harder? This is the type of a youngster I was. And I was-- as a little boy, I remember I was rather frightened. I don't know. I remember myself being very frightened. Although I had a lot of friends, so I'm not sure what kind of a kid I was. But then later on, I had been in a circle of friends and where play was a little more important than work. And we were this kind of a wealthy class of the Jews in Poland, in Lodz. And we were-- a lot of playing around and so on. So this is what kind of a man I-- I started becoming at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of your favorite activities playing-wise? What would the kids--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, going to nightclubs and to dancing. I was a very good dancer. I danced in some dance contests in those days. Those days were before today's music, so it was tangos and so on. And as a matter of fact, right now my dancing partner is right here in-- in this area, in Westchester. She came for a wedding of her-- of her daughter. So these-- these times were some drinking-- not too much-- but some drinking. I remember going out of town on a drinking bout and so on. But this was the playing. This was the playing. We, of course, we didn't have as many possibilities to amuse ourselves as there are available now. There was no television. The movies were in the beginning really, you know. I remember my first sound movie, for example. The first Jolson movie that came to Poland. You know, all these things were just developing. I remember the first radio that was in Lodz in a museum. So my life span, things have changed. You know, my life span went through a-- my life went through tremendous changes in-- in the way people live.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of neighborhood did you grow up in Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was a very specific neighborhood where some of the industrialists and professional people-- Jewish people-- were. So that when we went out, we had favorite street, let's say, where we were walking as youngsters. Not youngsters, as teenagers and later on. All people that I-- I-- I would see were people that I knew. It was a feeling of being in a place that everybody knew you, and you knew everybody. And it was a very different feeling than being brought up in a very big city. It wasn't a small city. It was city of, I think, 300,000 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your neighborhood mostly Jewish or mixed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly Jewish, mostly Jewish. But it was not the-- the poor Jews, you know? It was different. We were very assimilated. It was a very assimilated group of Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe your family's financial and social situation? Were you comfortable? Was there ever a point where you were struggling?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not to my mind. Although, this was the time of the Depression in the world. I remember the Depression as something we talked about, but we never felt anything-- any of it. No, we were rather-- rather well off.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that there were factories belonging to the family behind your residence.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a business that was pretty much Jewish run, or did you have Gentile employees?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Gentile employees, of course. All the workers were Gentiles, were Poles. It was a very-- a very different classification because the very skillful people who were needed in this type of business-- it was textiles-- were Germans. Were Germans who lived in-- in Lodz. And the lower classes were Poles. The office was mostly Jewish and Pole-- Jews and Polish, Polish people. So you know, there was a little of everything. But the textiles industry in Lodz, which Lodz was the industrial city of Poland, was mostly in the Jewish hands. The industry, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about your school days?

speaker: interviewee
text: My school days were-- as I told you what kind of a kid I was that I always could do better. Thinking back of it, they were very happy. They were very good. You know, I was very secure and so on. I had trouble with some teachers like every kid. And I had trouble with some stories but nothing specific. It was-- it was a good life. I-- the life-- my school was a very peculiar school. It was a very Polish school, but it was 90% or 95% Jewish kids. We had one or two Gentile per class.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a secular school. It wasn't a religious school.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, it was a secular Polish school. And it was, of course--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it public or private?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was private school, expensive private school. We had, by law, we had to have religion in schools. But the religion was for the Catholics. That was so the Jews had one or two years-- for one or two years had also to go to religion. Religion, to the class, to-- to take religion. Other years, we were just playing basketball, and Catholics had to go to religion. So no, religion was taught in schools, you know, by law.

speaker: interviewer
text: With a proportion of so many Jews and so few Gentiles, were relations between the two groups fairly comfortable?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very comfortable. Yes, very comfortable. Those people were my friends. You know, very close friends. I don't know what happened. I know that my closest friend-- who was a Gentile, a Catholic and whose father was fighting against-- against the-- the anti-Semitic excesses in Poland-- all of a sudden disappeared during the war. I have no idea. I've tried to find him. I don't know where he is. And I didn't find him. So whether he doesn't want to get in touch, whether he survived or not, I don't know. But he was a very close friend of mine, and I loved him very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Spoleczna Polski Gymnazium [POLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a gymnazium type school?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was lyceum, yes. Gymnazium. In other words, after-- after finishing 11 years of school or 11 years, you were given a certificate of-- we call it certificate of maturity. That was the name. And that entitled us to go into colleges and to universities.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go when you finished?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to University of Krakow after a year in France. I spent one year in France. I said to my father, I want to learn something about textiles. And I enrolled in Lyon in a school of textiles. After a few months there, I got bored by it. And I-- I-- I wrote to my father, find me a job with some of the industrialists. I wanted-- I want a hands-on experience. And he found me such a job in a very small town between Lyon and Grenoble. And these were good times in my life. [LAUGHS] I was there for several months, and I was learning how to build textile looms. When I came back, I decided I don't want to work in family business, because I didn't like very much my two uncles who were in it. And I decided I'd go to work outside. And I go to University of Krakow to study law, and I did that. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: There wasn't a problem with quotas for Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in the law school, no. In-- I wanted to be a medical doctor, by the way. But that was impossible because there was an entrance exam, which they made specifically so easy that everybody would pass. And then they would pick everybody else, but the Jews were not admitted at all. So most of the Jewish people had to study-- Jewish doctors had to study abroad. They went to France. They went to Italy and studied medicine. And for me, I didn't want to go there. I wanted to work, and I wanted to be in my city. I had a nice social life there, and I didn't want to leave that. So I started working for a banking house in Lodz, and going to Krakow just for the exams and passing the exams there, you know. So this was the extent of my studies until I came to the United States. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So right before the war, you were enrolled in university? You were studying?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I completed the studies just before the war, and I would have been a bachelor of law. And then I would have to go into what is called the practice, kind of. You work as-- you clerk for a judge. Then you clerk for another lawyer and so on to become a lawyer in Poland. But the war intervened. So I didn't do any of this clerking or anything like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: In between your studies, your work, and your playing, did you ever join any organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was a very-- very Socialist minded in those days. I was in the Socialists-- Young Socialists Group in Lodz. And I had a specific job for them. I was a poetry reader. You know, I had, already at that time, already wanted to be in front of an audience and-- and read poetry. So I was reading all those very revolutionary poems. And I also remember walking in a 1st of May parade of workers with the workers from my family's factory. Which you know, probably didn't endear me to my uncles any more than I liked them before. But that's, yeah. That's the main organization that I belonged to.

speaker: interviewer
text: In growing up in Lodz in this comfortable environment, what made you feel Jewish? Was your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family was not religious at all. My mother was absolutely against religion. She was this kind of a revolutionary who you know, was doing it-- I guess she was doing it to my father. She was giving lunches-- that's what I heard. That was before I was born. Lunches on Yom Kippur, you know, for her friends who couldn't eat at home. My father maintained the closeness with Judaism, but much more national closeness than religious. Although he would go to the temple on New Year and on Yom Kippur. But otherwise, you know, we'd celebrate Passover at home. We would have a Seder and so on. But otherwise, I didn't feel very much Jewish at that time. My Jewishness was imposed from the outside. I was told I am Jewish by people who did not consider me a Pole.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you give an example of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I knew that-- that I cannot do certain things. I cannot go to certain places because I am Jewish, because there were restrictions. Like what I told you about the medical school and so on. That's what I called it. They told me I am a Jew. I am different. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a negative thing to be Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a negative thing to be Jewish, in so far as you were concerned that you're living as a Pole. And you have to understand that I was brought up in a Polish culture. I was brought up with a very strong-- and Polish-- Poland between the wars, between the two World Wars was tremendously patriotic, you know. Because you know, they were occupied by the three powers around Poland for very-- for 150 years. And here they regained the independence. It was tremendous upsurge of-- of patriotic feeling. And we went with it. We were brought up in this culture. The Polish poems still speak to me in a different way than-- than other poems. And therefore, at this point, it was negative. It was making me different than everybody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that your father had Zionist sympathies.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you, as a boy or a young adult, ever see the appeal of Zionism?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not-- I have to be truthful about it. No, I did not. All I was thinking about is that I want to fight for Poland and so on. Our approach to it is that it's a bad mother, but it's a mother. Poland is a mother. It's a bad mother, yes. She doesn't like us. She doesn't love us, but it's a mother. And this is the way our group of Jews lived. It's-- you know, very similar to the way that the German Jews were after Haskalah, you know, the-- the-- the period of Enlightenment. They were Germans primarily. And this is the truth, you know. Today, I feel very differently. And I felt differently during the war. But that's the way I felt as a boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any experiences with anti-Semitism? Either name-calling or physical attacks as you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not growing up but at the university. It was the first time. In other words, getting out of my cocoon in Lodz, where I lived in this particular group of people, I didn't encounter anti-Semitism there. Getting to the university was the first time that I've seen two lines of Gentiles waiting outside the auditorium that we have been let in at a lecture. And walking out-- all the Jewish people walking out were beaten.

speaker: interviewer
text: You included?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was lucky. I was blonde and tall. And they didn't-- they didn't-- they let me go through. I didn't look like a Jew. So you know, at that point, I started getting stronger and stronger feelings of belonging to the Jewish nation. And you know, but I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anything--

speaker: interviewee
text: --still didn't know enough about Jews, and Judaism, and so on to really feel a very strong connection with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what could Jews do in a situation like you just described when they're sort of surrounded and get beaten? Was there anything, just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely nothing. Absolutely nothing could be done. We would just have to take it. If you wanted to become a lawyer or something like that, you just had to take it. There were separate benches. All right. I had this friend of mine that I described as Gentile. He sat down on benches with the Jews. But then he was beaten. You understand? So what could we do? We're a very small minority. And politically, we tried to support people that were not this way, that were not as anti-Semitic. You know, in Poland, anti-Semitism at that time was very strong. Was still Poland is very Catholic country. And the church at that time, especially on the level of parish, parish level, was extremely anti-Semitic. And that's how it-- you know, that's how it originated there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just to go back a little bit. You're describing how difficult it could be to feel positive about your Jewish identity in this kind of environment. Were there any things like food or customs that you can associate with your childhood? I mean, were there certain foods you had in your house that were identifiably Jewish that you were fond of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Let's say, potato pancakes or things like that. Yes, of course, we had. I also-- I heard Yiddish spoken by my grandparents to my-- to my mother, for example.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you understand it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't, no. And my parents never spoke Yiddish to me. But my mother knew Yiddish, so you know, I heard these things. So there was some culture. Look, the very beginning, once I-- once I was captured-- later on, I will tell you how much-- how much pride I had in Judaism already. And I didn't want to tell anybody that I'm not Jewish. I didn't want to lie when I had the possibility.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that you know, it-- it went very fast that I reverted to-- to that. And later on, you'll find out that I-- I went at it very seriously and tried to learn a lot about what Judaism is, and Jewish history, and so on. OK. So on the eve of the German march into Lodz, you're doing university studies in law and working part-time. And you have family in Lodz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My primary family-- primary family was not exactly-- was not in Lodz at that point. At this particular point, my father was in New York. And my sister Mary, the younger sister, with her brother were-- with her husband were in New York. So that when the war broke out, my older sister was living in Lodz. My mother and I were living in Lodz. My father and my sister were visiting in the-- in here, in America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
